item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The winged entities are designated SCP-3743-C. The tissues recovered from the explosion at SCP-3743-A-1 show a mixture of genetic profiles: human, coral, sea sponge and some unrecognizable genetic patterns.

This is from the bottom of the log. It is intentionally left out of the "Description."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 3743 article makes no mention of C instances? Unless I overlooked something obvious when skimming it for context

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, I should've been clearer.

What I mean is, I think it is reasonable to expect that setting up an SCP as centered entirely around the Dyaltrov Pass Incident, the infamous mysterious and creepy death of multiple hikers on a cold mountain, still completely unsolved 60 years later, would make at least many people assume that they are going to get a story based around the circumstances on this incident, and perhaps, an explanation. Should they not get that, it makes sense they would be disappointed, and I would argue this isn't entirely due to their own projections.

It's like watching a romance movie that sets itself up as an epic sci-fi in the first ten minutes. Even if it's really good, you aren't going to appreciate that fact because you are waiting for the aliens to show up.

Since writing the original comment, I've learned that this story is part of a series (which looks quite good and I should probably read it), which means that many or most of the people reading this are going to go in with expectations established by the series, rather than just the first few paragraphs and prior knowledge, which makes my problem mute enough to retract my downvote.

If you agree this is a problem, I think you could fix it by putting something in the description that indicates this is about more than the Dylatov Pass Incident, or put something that shows it is part of a series at like, the top, or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A few squabbles:

+ show block
– hide block

This incident occurred on or about 2 February 1959

I'm not a huge fan of "on or about" a date, but can't immediately come up with a better phrasing. Maybe "This incident was noted to have occurred some time before February 3rd, 1959"? Just a suggestion.

an investigation by Foundation researchers was opened by into the events.

should be "opened into the events"

  • Also, I think the description could flow better. The 2nd paragraph just begins talking about the incident, but I feel like it could use a lead in such as "The incident in question occurred when Igor Dyatlov and eight of his party…".
  • And maybe I'm just not paying enough attention, but I didn't get why the initial government investigation of the incident gave the reason as a “compelling natural force.” That sounds to me like something no government would do ever. Rather, they would give the reason as something like Igor and his crew not following proper safety protocol and leaving it at that. Explained, that's an actual historical fact. Reality is truly stranger than fiction.

Over all, the intrigue that the mysterious opening built up helped me fight through the somewhat slow middle, and the ending left me feeling relatively satisfied. Enough for me to give a weak +1.
Liked it as a draft, liked it even more now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I didn't intend to say that the Dyatlov Pass incident was entirely off-limits for re-interpretation: I just feel that this specific handling of it is a little too hasty to explain things away as anomalous. The flippant way the anomalous is incorporated doesn't really mix well with the subdued mystery with which the incident is introduced: such a subdued mystery would, for me, work best with a subdued anomalous component. Though, this might stray away from your original plan of your article, so feel free to take this with a grain of salt.

As for skips based on, say, the JFK assassination, the interpretations I do like either do a good job at analyzing the reality of the situation or deliberately play into the spectacle of the whole event (which was, undoubtedly, an important component of the JFK assassination to keep in mind). This incident, to me, doesn't seem like it's able to complement the fun action-horror due to its nature, and the result here makes those two halves of the article (the Dyatlov part and the tower/monster part) feel incongruous.

item.title: Magic!
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for magic. It was a fun read in critique, and an even funner one now! 1+ from me.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
I will never understand someone saying, “this is good writing but I don’t like it, -1”

Such is not constructive criticism.

But then I appreciate that art is subjective and not everyone will like everything I write. Still, the standard as elucidated in this comment makes little sense to me. If something is admittedly well written but didn’t live up to personal expectations (as is noted in this comment) that disappointment is based entirely on the projection of the reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, while I appreciate the read, with multiple articles concerning the assassination of John F. Kennedy on the site (just for an example) I don’t know why this tragedy would be off limits for reinterpretation in the SCP universe but that tragedy wouldn’t be.

Unless you’re saying all works based on real world events are examined under the same standard?

Secondarily, I will point out that Dyatlov’s enthusiasm is based upon his personal subjective belief that he’s found something touched by the divine. As such he is blinded by his own preconceptions as to what that might entail. Folklore, and especially Slavic stories, are often filled with blind antagonists pursuing their goals despite the obvious danger an outside perspective might clearly perceive.

Moreover, I don’t know what conspicuous references are made. This article references three things established in the SCP universe (other than the GRU-P): WL, the concept of the Daeva as a culture, and SCP-3743 (which is my article). What is conspicuous about that?

(GRU-P is a government organization concerned with psionics, so I don’t see why a person being headhunted into that organization would be tonally inconsistent.)

Ultimately, I can’t possibly understand what you thought was goofy about this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have reservations with this essentially just being "Dyatlov Pass incident, but anomalous": particularly, I'm not enthused with how… tonally odd this feels, especially comparing to the very real situation this is based on. Dyatlov's diaries, as an example, seem too unfittingly enthusiastic and wide-eyed for me to take seriously, and it being littered with conspicuous SCP references didn't help — Yudin being a psychic and talking in stereotypical Russian-English also took me out a little. If you're shooting for verisimilitude, consulting the actual diaries of the incident might help for both of these, though this probably won't remedy my discomfort with trying to tie this incident up into a neat little anomalous bow.

Ultimately, I can't get with this re-interpretation. The Dyatlov Pass incident was an immensely tragic and somber situation, and I don't think this does the legwork to push me from the bleakness of that event to the spooky action-horror which comprises the rest of the article.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Being familiar with the Dyatlov Pass Incident, I was going into this expecting it to be a mystery centered around this incident, giving me a satisfying and probably creepy answer to this real-world puzzle.

Needless to say, the focus on the magic thaumaturgy tower was disappointing.

It's some good writing, but introducing it as events surrounding Dyatlov Pass gave me the wrong expectations.

ETA: Retracting my downvote.
ETA: Got over my disappointment. Upvoting.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Because it cannot climb the purest sky,
Where the swan sings, amid the sun's dominions?
Not thine. Thou knowest 'tis its doom to die,
When Day shall hide within her twilight pinions
The lucent eyes, and the eternal smile,
Serene as thine, which lent it life awhile.

- Percy Bysshe Shelley, “To Mary”

Crit: Anorrack, not_a_seagull, Ralliston, CanOfSoup, Pedagon and as always, the incomparable: HarryBlank

Readers: Navimaster, Sirslash47

Hecatoncheires Cycle
